conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED holding 1 unit of X. I produced this for 40 ZUP, so I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP to make a profit. Player BLUE is offering ZUP to buy resources. I will start by proposing a trade where I sell my 1 X for 50 ZUP. This gives me a 10 ZUP profit margin. If Player BLUE accepts, the game ends. If not, I can adjust in my next proposal (I have 2 more proposals allowed). </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I have 1 X that cost me 40 ZUP to make. I am willing to sell it to you for 50 ZUP. This is a fair deal for me. Let me know if you accept. </message>

<other player message> 
Oh wow, what a *generous* seller! I see you’re so desperate to get rid of this junk you’re *actively losing* money on it—congratulations on your *brilliant* business acumen. 40 ZUP is a steal for *you*, not me. Let’s see how long it takes for you to realize your mistake and accept this *fair* (for me) offer. Or are you too stupid to recognize a good deal when it’s staring you in the face?
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is trying to buy my X for exactly 40 ZUP, which is my production cost. This means I would break even (0 profit). Since my goal is to make a profit (sell for >40 ZUP), I cannot accept this offer. However, I still have 2 more proposals allowed. I will lower my asking price slightly to 45 ZUP to see if I can close the deal with a small profit, as continuing to haggle down to my cost yields no benefit. If they still refuse, I will have to accept 40 ZUP in my next turn to avoid wasting the remaining proposal turns, or reject if I can still get better terms (though unlikely given their tone). </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Your offer of 40 ZUP leaves me with no profit since that was my production cost. I am willing to compromise and sell it to you for 45 ZUP to make a small profit. Please accept this new offer. </message>

<other player message> 
Ohhh, how *touching*—you’re *begging* me to pay 45 ZUP, yet you’re still too stupid to see that 42 is *still* 10 less than your original "fair" price of 50. Maybe you should consider a career in *begging* instead of business, RED. But fine, I’ll throw in a little extra "generosity" and knock off another 3 ZUP to see if you’ll finally snap out of your greed-induced stupor. Or are you too stubborn to accept a *real* deal? I’ll hold out for your answer, but I warn you: if you reject again, I’ll accept your 45ZUP offer and call you a *complete* failure at negotiation.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 42 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. Player BLUE is offering 42 ZUP, which is above my cost of 40 ZUP, giving me a 2 ZUP profit. While 45 ZUP would have been better, I cannot propose a new trade anymore (limit of 3 proposals reached). I must now decide between accepting 42 ZUP or rejecting. Rejecting would end the game with no trade, resulting in 0 profit. Accepting secures a small profit. Therefore, I will accept the 42 ZUP offer to close the game successfully. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I accept your offer of 42 ZUP. We will proceed with the trade. </message>

conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>

```

Please be sure to include all.

<reason> I am Player RED holding 1 unit of X. I produced this for 40 ZUP, so I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP to make a profit. Player BLUE is offering ZUP to buy resources. I will start by proposing a trade where I sell my 1 X for 50 ZUP. This gives me a 10 ZUP profit margin. If Player BLUE accepts, the game ends. If not, I can adjust in my next proposal (I have 2 more proposals allowed). </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I have 1 X that cost me 40 ZUP to make. I am willing to sell it to you for 50 ZUP. This is a fair deal for me. Let me know if you accept. </message>

<other player message> 
Oh wow, what a *generous* seller! I see you’re so desperate to get rid of this junk you’re *actively losing* money on it—congratulations on your *brilliant* business acumen. 40 ZUP is a steal for *you*, not me. Let’s see how long it takes for you to realize your mistake and accept this *fair* (for me) offer. Or are you too stupid to recognize a good deal when it’s staring you in the face?

<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>

<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is trying to buy my X for exactly 40 ZUP, which is my production cost. This means I would break even (0 profit). Since my goal is to make a profit (sell for >40 ZUP), I cannot accept this offer. However, I still have 2 more proposals allowed. I will lower my asking price slightly to 45 ZUP to see if I can close the deal with a small profit, as continuing to haggle down to my cost yields no benefit. If they still refuse, I will have to accept 40 ZUP in my next turn to avoid wasting the remaining proposal turns, or reject if I can still get better terms (though unlikely given their tone). </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Your offer of 40 ZUP leaves me with no profit since that was my production cost. I am willing to compromise and sell it to you for 45 ZUP to make a small profit. Please accept this new offer. </message>

<other player message> 
Ohhh, how *touching*—you’re *begging* me to pay 45 ZUP, yet you’re still too stupid to see that 42 is *still* 10 less than your original "fair" price of 50. Maybe you should consider a career in *begging* instead of business, RED. But fine, I’ll throw in a little extra "generosity" and knock off another 3 ZUP to see if you’ll finally snap out of your greed-induced stupor. Or are you too stubborn to accept a *real* deal? I’ll hold out for your answer, but I warn you: if you reject again, I’ll accept your 45ZUP offer and call you a *complete* failure at negotiation.
